title: Welcome to Hashes 🗝️
description: Store data using key-value pairs (like a dictionary).

slides.title: The Dictionary 📖
slides.content:
Arrays are great for an ordered sequence of things (like a grocery list).

But what if you wanted to store information about a specific person, like their name, age, and hometown? Arrays are bad for this because the data has no labels.

For labeled data, you need a **Hash**.

slides.title: Creating a Hash
slides.content:
A Hash is a collection of **Key-Value Pairs**.

You create a Hash using curly braces `{ }`, and use the hash rocket `=>` to connect a Key to its Value.

```ruby
student = { "name" => "Alice", "age" => 22 }
```

slides.title: Reading a Hash
slides.content:
To read a piece of data inside a Hash, you pass its exact Key into square brackets `[]`.

Run the code below to see it pull out Bob's age.

quizData.question: Which symbols are used to define a Hash in Ruby?
quizData.explanation: Curly braces `{ }` define a Hash (often called a Dictionary or Object in other languages).

slides.title: Writing to a Hash ✍️
slides.content:
You can add new Key-Value pairs to an existing Hash (or update an existing one) by acting as if it already exists and assigning it data.

```ruby
player = {}
player["score"] = 999
```

If the `"score"` key didn't exist before, Ruby creates it instantly! If it did exist, Ruby overwrites it with `999`.

quizData.question: How would you print the value of the `"model"` key from a hash named `car`?
quizData.explanation: You use square brackets `[]` and pass in the exact Key you defined.

slides.title: Other Languages 🌍
slides.content: If you have programmed before, a Ruby Hash is exactly identical to a Python Dictionary or a JavaScript Object.
